title: Pakistan-Afghanistan Peace Talks Open in Urumqi, China
detail: Senior officials from Pakistan and Afghanistan convened in Urumqi, northwestern China, for the first formal round of ceasefire talks since the post-Ramadan truce expired. China brokered the session. Afghanistan's Foreign Ministry spokesperson Abdul Qahar Balkhi confirmed a 'mid-level delegation' traveled to Urumqi. Pakistan's Foreign Ministry stated: 'The burden of a real process lies with Afghanistan, which must demonstrate visible and verifiable action against terrorist groups using Afghan soil against Pakistan.' Earlier mediation attempts had been held in Qatar and Turkey. Talks came even as Pakistani shelling of Kunar, Paktika, and Khost continued simultaneously, highlighting the fragility of the diplomatic process.

title: Pakistan Launches Sustained Mortar, Rocket and Drone Strikes on Three Eastern Afghan Provinces
detail: Pakistan launched sustained mortar, rocket, and drone strikes across three eastern Afghan provinces — Kunar, Paktika, and Khost — on April 2. Taliban deputy spokesman Hamdullah Fitrat stated Pakistan had been 'continuously carrying out mortar, missile and drone attacks' targeting civilian areas. In Kunar's Sarkano and Manogai districts, over 150 long-range artillery shells were fired. Paktika's Shkin district and Khost's Zazi Maidan district were also hit. The strikes killed two children and wounded approximately 30 people, mostly civilians. Afghan officials accused Pakistan of intentionally targeting civilian areas; Pakistan asserted its operations targeted TTP and Taliban military infrastructure only.

title: Torkham Partially Reopens for Afghan Repatriation; 705 Returned First Day
detail: Pakistan partially reopened the Torkham border crossing on April 2 for the limited purpose of repatriating detained Afghan nationals. On the first day of operations, 705 individuals from 223 families were sent back to Afghanistan. General trade and civilian movement remained suspended following the March 27 Afghan sniper incident that wounded a Pakistani Frontier Corps Subedar. Pakistani immigration officials stated that only registered Afghan deportees would be permitted to cross, and that the crossing could be closed again at any time. Afghan officials described the mass repatriations as forced displacement during an active conflict, calling them a humanitarian concern.
